13: A type: 'And the Spirit of the LORD rushed upon David from that day forward.' David's anointing by Samuel is the OT's paradigmatic messianic event — the word mashiach (anointed one) derives its ultimate meaning from this moment. David is anointed as YHWH's chosen king; the Spirit comes upon him as the empowering for his royal vocation. Jesus's baptism is the typological fulfillment: he is anointed (the meaning of christos, the Greek equivalent of mashiach) by the Spirit who descends and remains (John 1:32-33). The crucial difference: the Spirit rushed upon David from that day forward, but left Saul (1 Samuel 16:14); on Jesus the Spirit descends and remains — the permanent anointing that makes him the Messiah whose Spirit-empowered rule will never end.
1: YHWH asks Samuel: 'How long will you mourn for Saul, since I have rejected him from being king over Israel? Fill your horn with oil and go. I will send you to Jesse the Bethlehemite, for I have provided for myself a king among his sons.' The divine redirect from mourning to mission is the pattern of prophetic recommissioning after loss. Prolonged mourning for what YHWH has ended becomes an obstacle to what YHWH is beginning. Christ applies the same principle: 'Let the dead bury their own dead; but as for you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God' (Luke 9:60). The capacity to release what YHWH has ended is the prerequisite for receiving what YHWH is beginning.
2: Samuel fears Saul and objects: 'If Saul hears, he will kill me.' The prophet who just executed Agag before YHWH is afraid to anoint a new king because of Saul's surveillance. YHWH provides a cover story: take a heifer for sacrifice, invite Jesse, and in that context perform the anointing. The practical wisdom of the cover — a legitimate cultic act that creates a plausible reason for Samuel's presence at Bethlehem — is not deception but prudential protection of a divine mission. Christ similarly moved through hostile territory on God's timing: 'his hour had not yet come' (John 7:30).
3: YHWH will show Samuel what to do at Bethlehem; he will show him the one to anoint. The step-by-step divine guidance — go first, I will instruct you further once you arrive — is the model of prophetic commission: YHWH gives enough guidance for the next step without necessarily disclosing the whole mission in advance. Abraham received the same kind of guidance ('go to the land I will show you'); the apostles in Acts receive it step by step. Faith acts on revealed next-steps rather than waiting for complete disclosure.
4: Samuel does as YHWH commanded and comes to Bethlehem. The elders of the city tremble at his coming and ask: 'Do you come peaceably?' A traveling prophet of Samuel's authority and recent history (the Saul confrontation) arriving without warning was both an honor and a potential threat. The elders' question is reasonable: prophetic visits in the narrative tradition often preceded divine judgment on a community.
5: Samuel assures them he comes peaceably and consecrates Jesse and his sons for the sacrificial meal. The consecration (wayyəqaddēš) of the family before the meal ensures proper ritual preparation. The sacred meal that will provide cover for the anointing is itself conducted with full cultic integrity — the cover story is a real event, not merely a pretense. The anointing will happen within a genuinely holy context.
6: When Eliab enters, Samuel thinks: 'Surely YHWH's anointed is before him.' Eliab is tall, firstborn, and impressive — exactly the kind of man Israel chose when they asked for a king 'like all the nations.' Samuel's immediate assumption that the impressive eldest son is YHWH's choice shows that even the prophet who anointed Saul on the basis of his impressive appearance has not yet fully internalized the corrective lesson. The same evaluative framework that produced Saul is Samuel's initial response to Eliab.
7: 'Do not look on his appearance or on the height of his stature, because I have rejected him. For YHWH sees not as man sees; man looks on the outward appearance, but YHWH looks on the heart (lēḇāḇ).' The most famous statement of divine evaluation in Samuel is simultaneously a correction of Samuel's seeing and a theological principle for all human evaluation of YHWH's choices. Christ applies the same principle against surface-level religious evaluation: 'Do not judge by appearances, but judge with right judgment' (John 7:24). Paul's application — 'we no longer know Christ according to the flesh' (2 Corinthians 5:16) — means YHWH's way of seeing has become the standard for covenant community evaluation.
8: Jesse calls Abinadab and passes him before Samuel; YHWH has not chosen him. The rejection of son after son — without external explanation — requires Samuel to wait through YHWH's sovereign process. The repeated 'YHWH has not chosen this one' is the prophetic experience of divine selection that exceeds human evaluation categories. YHWH's negative is not arbitrary; the positive will be explained only in retrospect, in David's subsequent career.
9: Shammah passes before Samuel; YHWH has not chosen him. The third rejection completes the triple-rejection pattern (cf. vv6-9) that establishes the principle definitively: the obvious candidates are not YHWH's choice. The pattern of YHWH bypassing the expected and choosing the unexpected is the structural template for the incarnation: 'Can anything good come out of Nazareth?' (John 1:46).
10: Jesse makes seven of his sons pass before Samuel, and Samuel says: 'YHWH has not chosen any of these.' The exhaustion of the obvious candidates — all seven present sons rejected — forces the disclosure of the absent one. The process of elimination that makes the hidden son visible is the narrative technique that heightens the unexpected nature of YHWH's choice. Seven sons present, none chosen; the eighth, absent, is the one.
11: Samuel asks: 'Are all your sons here?' Jesse acknowledges the youngest, who is tending the sheep. 'Send and get him, for we will not sit down until he comes here.' The youngest tending sheep is the classic insignificance-position in ancient family structures: the shepherd-son is at the bottom of the inheritance order and on the periphery of the family's social world. YHWH's anointed is found at the sheep. The pattern — shepherd called to be king — is explicitly applied to Christ in John 10:11 and fulfilled in the Good Shepherd who is also the messianic King.
12: The young man is brought in: ruddy (ʾaḏmônî), with beautiful eyes and handsome appearance. YHWH says: 'Arise, anoint him, for this is he.' David's appearance is not irrelevant — it is simply not the basis for YHWH's choice. The difference from Saul: Saul's appearance was the primary thing noted and the thing Israel emphasized; David's appearance is noted after YHWH has already declared his choice. YHWH chooses by heart; he does not choose the ugly. The incarnate Christ likewise had 'no beauty that we should desire him' (Isaiah 53:2) in suffering — the kingly beauty appeared in resurrection (Revelation 1:13-16).
14: The Spirit of YHWH departs from Saul and a harmful spirit from YHWH terrifies him. The judicial withdrawal of the Spirit — replacing the divine empowerment with a divine affliction — is the covenant consequence of persistent disobedience. The 'evil spirit from YHWH' (rûaḥ rāʿāh mēʾiṯ YHWH) is not demonic possession in the later technical sense but YHWH's judicial abandonment leaving Saul vulnerable to psychological deterioration. Hebrews 6:4-6 warns that those who have 'tasted the heavenly gift' and then 'fallen away' make restoration impossible — Saul's condition is the OT prototype of this apostasy warning. The Spirit that came on him in 1 Samuel 10:6-10 and made him 'another man' is now withdrawn.
15: Saul's servants observe his condition and tell him a harmful spirit from God is troubling him. The servants' diagnosis is accurate — they correctly identify the spirit's divine origin rather than attributing it to natural causes. Their therapeutic proposal (music to soothe the spirit) reflects the ANE understanding of music as spiritually efficacious — not merely emotionally calming but capable of addressing spiritual disturbance. The remedy they propose will introduce the one who will succeed Saul into his very household.
16: The servants recommend finding a man skilled at playing the lyre. When the harmful spirit from God is upon Saul, the musician will play and Saul will be well. The servant's proposal is practical and YHWH's providence is operating through it: the most natural human solution to a spiritual problem will be the instrument for installing YHWH's anointed in the house of the rejected king. Providence typically operates through the natural and ordinary.
17: Saul instructs his servants to find him a man who plays well and bring him. The king who has been rejected and is now troubled by an evil spirit is actively seeking the one who will displace him — unaware of David's identity or anointing. The irony is deep: Saul seeks relief from his affliction; YHWH sends the one whose arrival will make Saul's condition permanent. Saul's request creates the opening that installs his successor in his own house.
18: A servant knows of David son of Jesse: 'a man skillful in playing, a man of valor, a warrior, prudent in speech, a man of good presence, and YHWH is with him.' The servant's enumeration of David's qualities — musical skill, valor, eloquence, presence, divine favor — is the most comprehensive character summary in the David narrative to this point. The final quality — 'YHWH is with him' (YHWH ʿimmô) — is the defining one. It is the same quality that will characterize David throughout his career and that Mary's angel announces for Jesus: 'YHWH is with you' (Luke 1:28).
19: Saul sends to Jesse asking for David. The royal command to Jesse — 'send me David your son, who is with the sheep' — is the official summons that moves David from the shepherd field to the royal court. The transition from shepherd to royal service will be completed when he enters Saul's house (v21). The path from Bethlehem's sheep to Jerusalem's throne runs through the house of the man who will eventually seek his life.
20: Jesse sends David to Saul with a donkey, bread, a skin of wine, and a young goat — a gift appropriate for a royal introduction. The provisions are the household gift of a Judahite farmer sending his son to court service: not extravagant but adequate. The contrast between the donkeys Kish sent with Saul (1 Samuel 9:3) to find what was lost and the donkey Jesse sends with David is the narrative's quiet bookend: one journey ended with a king-in-waiting meeting his anointer; this journey begins a king-in-waiting's service to the rejected king.
21: David comes to Saul and enters his service; Saul loves him greatly and David becomes his armor-bearer. The anointed future king serves as the personal attendant of the rejected current king — the kenotic structure of David's position in Saul's court is the OT type of what Paul describes in Philippians 2:7: Christ 'took the form of a servant' and 'humbled himself.' The greatest will serve among them as the least (Luke 22:26-27). David's armor-bearer role will produce the military opportunity for Goliath (1 Samuel 17) without removing the humility of the service.
22: Saul sends to Jesse asking David to remain permanently in his service, 'for he has found favor in my sight.' The favor David finds in Saul's eyes (māṣāʾ ḥēn bəʿênāyw) is YHWH's hand at work — the same formula applied to Joseph in Potiphar's house (Genesis 39:4), to Moses before Pharaoh (Exodus 3:21), and ultimately fulfilled in Christ who 'increased in favor with God and man' (Luke 2:52). Those anointed by YHWH find favor precisely where they serve, even when serving those who will eventually oppose them.
23: Whenever the evil spirit from God is upon Saul, David takes the lyre and plays with his hand, and Saul is refreshed and well, and the evil spirit departs from him. The musician whose playing drives away the spirit that afflicts the rejected king is the pastoral type of Christ's ministry to the spiritually oppressed — the ministry Isaiah describes as 'binding up the brokenhearted' and 'proclaiming liberty to the captives' (Isaiah 61:1, cited by Jesus in Luke 4:18). David's music is the prototype of the ministry of the Anointed who brings relief to those afflicted by spiritual oppression. The Spirit that departed from Saul when David was anointed (v14) departs from Saul again when David plays.
